name: GPU Cluster Provisioning
description: Setting up and configuring GPU clusters for AI workloads including hardware, OS, and software stack deployment
key_facts: NVIDIA Base Command Manager automates OS provisioning, driver installation, and software stack deployment on DGX clusters
PXE boot enables network-based OS installation across many cluster nodes simultaneously
Ansible and other configuration management tools automate consistent software deployment across GPU nodes
NVIDIA GPU Operator installs and manages GPU drivers, container toolkit, and device plugins in Kubernetes
Firmware updates for GPUs, NVSwitches, and ConnectX adapters should be coordinated to maintain compatibility
common_misconceptions: GPU driver installation is a one-time task (drivers must be regularly updated for security patches and performance improvements)
Standard OS provisioning tools handle GPU nodes without modification (GPU-specific drivers and libraries require additional configuration)

name: Multi-Tenant User Management
description: Managing multiple users and teams sharing GPU cluster resources
key_facts: Namespaces in Kubernetes isolate resources between different teams and projects
GPU quotas limit the maximum number of GPUs each team or user can consume
LDAP and Active Directory integration provides centralized authentication for cluster access
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) restricts user permissions based on their role in the organization
Fair-share scheduling ensures equitable GPU access across teams based on configured priorities
common_misconceptions: GPU quotas guarantee GPU availability (quotas set limits but don't reserve resources; reservation requires separate mechanisms)
A single admin account is sufficient for cluster management (RBAC and per-user accounts are essential for security and accountability)

name: Slurm Job Scheduling for GPU Clusters
description: Using Slurm workload manager for scheduling and managing GPU-accelerated AI jobs
key_facts: Slurm is the most widely used job scheduler for HPC and AI GPU clusters
GRES (Generic Resources) in Slurm enables GPU-aware scheduling with --gres=gpu:N syntax
Slurm partitions organize nodes into groups with different access policies and priorities
Job arrays enable launching multiple similar training runs with different hyperparameters
Slurm's accounting system tracks GPU-hours consumed by each user and project
common_misconceptions: Slurm automatically optimizes GPU utilization (jobs must be correctly configured to fully use allocated GPUs)
Interactive and batch jobs compete equally for resources (partitions and QOS settings typically prioritize production workloads)

name: Kubernetes GPU Scheduling
description: GPU-aware scheduling in Kubernetes for AI training and inference workloads
key_facts: NVIDIA device plugin for Kubernetes exposes GPU resources that pods can request via resource limits
NVIDIA Run:ai provides GPU orchestration with fractional GPU allocation, fairness, and queueing
Time-slicing allows multiple pods to share a single GPU by dividing GPU time between containers
MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) support in Kubernetes enables partitioning a single GPU into isolated instances for pods
Node affinity and taints/tolerations control which pods get scheduled on GPU nodes
common_misconceptions: Kubernetes natively understands GPU resources (the NVIDIA device plugin is required to expose GPUs as schedulable resources)
GPU time-slicing provides the same isolation as MIG (time-slicing shares GPU memory and provides no memory isolation)

name: DCGM GPU Monitoring
description: Comprehensive GPU monitoring and diagnostics using NVIDIA DCGM
key_facts: DCGM provides GPU health monitoring, diagnostics, and telemetry collection for data center environments
DCGM Exporter exposes GPU metrics in Prometheus format for integration with Grafana dashboards
Key DCGM metrics include GPU utilization, memory usage, temperature, power draw, PCIe throughput, and NVLink throughput
DCGM health checks detect GPU issues including ECC errors, thermal throttling, and hardware failures
DCGM supports policy-based actions that can trigger alerts or node draining when GPU health degrades
common_misconceptions: nvidia-smi provides the same capabilities as DCGM (DCGM adds health diagnostics, group management, and integration with monitoring systems)
GPU memory errors always indicate hardware failure (transient single-bit ECC errors are normal and automatically corrected)

name: GPU Cluster Troubleshooting
description: Diagnosing and resolving common GPU cluster issues including hardware failures, driver problems, and performance degradation
key_facts: Xid errors in the kernel log (dmesg) indicate GPU hardware or driver issues with specific error codes for each type
Double-bit ECC (uncorrectable) errors require replacing the affected GPU as data integrity is compromised
NVLink errors can cause training hangs and are diagnosed using nvidia-smi nvlink --status
GPU fallen off the bus errors typically indicate hardware failure, overheating, or power delivery issues
NCCL communication failures are often caused by InfiniBand link issues, firewall rules, or incorrect NCCL environment variables
common_misconceptions: All GPU errors require hardware replacement (many issues are resolved by driver updates, firmware upgrades, or configuration changes)
Training hangs are always caused by GPU issues (network problems, storage I/O, and software deadlocks are common causes)

name: GPU Container Management
description: Managing GPU-enabled containers for AI workloads including runtime configuration and registry management
key_facts: NVIDIA Container Toolkit configures Docker and containerd to pass GPU devices into containers
NGC private registries allow organizations to host custom GPU container images securely
Container environment variables like NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES control which GPUs are accessible inside a container
Multi-stage Docker builds reduce container image size by separating build and runtime dependencies
Container health checks validate that GPUs are accessible and functional when containers start
common_misconceptions: GPU containers need the NVIDIA driver installed inside the container (drivers are provided by the host; only CUDA toolkit is needed in the container)
Docker alone supports GPU passthrough (the NVIDIA Container Toolkit runtime is required to expose GPU devices)

name: NVIDIA GPU Operator for Kubernetes
description: Automating GPU software stack deployment and management in Kubernetes using the GPU Operator
key_facts: GPU Operator automates deployment of NVIDIA drivers, container toolkit, device plugin, and DCGM in Kubernetes
It uses Kubernetes Operator pattern with CRDs to manage the lifecycle of GPU software components
GPU Operator supports pre-installed drivers or can install drivers as container workloads
Node Feature Discovery (NFD) automatically labels GPU nodes with hardware capabilities
GPU Operator simplifies upgrades by managing driver and toolkit versions through the ClusterPolicy CRD
common_misconceptions: GPU Operator replaces manual GPU management entirely (custom configurations may still require manual intervention)
GPU Operator only works with specific Kubernetes distributions (it supports standard Kubernetes, OpenShift, Rancher, and others)

name: GPU Utilization Optimization
description: Strategies for maximizing GPU utilization and minimizing idle GPU time in shared clusters
key_facts: MIG partitioning allows running multiple small workloads on a single GPU to improve utilization
GPU time-slicing enables sharing a GPU across multiple containers with round-robin scheduling
Preemptable workloads can be interrupted to make room for higher-priority jobs, improving overall utilization
Job packing strategies fill GPU gaps by scheduling smaller jobs alongside large multi-GPU jobs
Average GPU utilization across a cluster is a key metric for assessing infrastructure efficiency
common_misconceptions: 100% GPU allocation equals 100% GPU utilization (allocated GPUs may sit idle due to I/O bottlenecks or poor workload design)
Oversubscription always improves efficiency (excessive oversubscription degrades performance for all workloads)

name: GPU Infrastructure Cost Management
description: Managing and optimizing the costs of GPU infrastructure for AI operations
key_facts: GPU-hour tracking per team and project enables chargeback and cost allocation
Idle GPU detection and automated reclamation prevent wasted resources
Right-sizing GPU allocations matches job requirements to available GPU types (MIG, full GPU, multi-GPU)
Scheduled scaling powers down or reduces GPU nodes during off-peak hours
Total cost of ownership (TCO) for GPU infrastructure includes hardware, power, cooling, networking, and software licensing
common_misconceptions: GPU hardware cost is the dominant expense (power, cooling, and networking can exceed hardware cost over the system lifetime)
Cloud GPUs are always more expensive than on-premises (for bursty workloads, cloud GPUs can be more cost-effective)
